title: The Future of Renewable Energy
subtitle: How solar, wind, and battery storage are reshaping global power markets
summary: Renewable energy capacity grew 24% in 2025, with solar accounting for more than half of all new installations worldwide. Battery storage costs fell below $80/kWh for the first time, making round-the-clock renewable power economically viable.

sections.title: The Future of Renewable Energy
sections.emphasis: Renewable energy capacity grew 24% in 2025, with solar accounting for more than half of all new installations worldwide.

items.text: Renewable energy capacity grew 24% in 2025, with solar accounting for more than half of all new installations worldwide.
Battery storage cost, below the $80 threshold for the first time.
Solar LCOE reaches parity with coal in most markets.
Battery storage costs fall below $120/kWh, enabling 4-hour storage.
Global renewable investment surpasses $1 trillion annually.
Battery costs cross the $80/kWh threshold; round-the-clock renewables become viable.

items.label: Solar
items.text: Dominates new installations; fastest declining cost curve.

items.label: Wind
items.text: Higher capacity factor but longer permitting timelines and higher upfront costs.

sections.title: Industry View
sections.emphasis: The era of fossil fuels as the baseload default is ending. The question is not if, but how fast.

items.text: The era of fossil fuels as the baseload default is ending. The question is not if, but how fast.
Grid operators must accelerate storage deployment to absorb variable supply.
Policy should focus on permitting reform and grid interconnection, not subsidies.
Emerging markets will leapfrog legacy grid infrastructure with distributed solar plus storage.
